heroOverview: Myopathy is a disease of skeletal muscle in which the primary pathology lies within the muscle fibre itself rather than in the nerve supplying it. Taken together, inherited and acquired myopathies affect approximately 1 in 1,000 people worldwide, though the true prevalence is higher because mild cases remain undiagnosed. The clinical hallmark is proximal limb-girdle weakness — difficulty rising from a chair, climbing stairs, or lifting the arms above the head — with relatively preserved reflexes and sensation, distinguishing it from neuropathy. ICD-10 G72.9 (myopathy, unspecified) is applied when muscle disease is confirmed but the subtype — inflammatory, hereditary, toxic, endocrine, or metabolic — has not yet been established. Identifying the specific cause is essential because inflammatory myopathies respond to immunosuppression, endocrine myopathies reverse with hormone correction, and toxic myopathies resolve when the causative drug is stopped.

keyStats.prevalence: All myopathies combined affect roughly 1 in 1,000 people; inflammatory myopathies alone affect approximately 22 per 100,000 adults (NIH estimate)
keyStats.demographics: Varies widely by type — Duchenne muscular dystrophy affects 1 in 3,500 male births; polymyositis and dermatomyositis peak in adults aged 40-60
keyStats.avgAge: Hereditary myopathies typically present in childhood or young adulthood; acquired myopathies more commonly in middle-aged and older adults
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated several million globally when all subtypes are combined; inflammatory myopathies alone account for tens of thousands of diagnoses annually in high-income countries

definition: Myopathy (ICD-10: G72.9) refers to any primary disease of skeletal muscle, characterised by weakness, wasting, or dysfunction arising from pathology intrinsic to the muscle fibre — not from its motor nerve or neuromuscular junction. The broad categories are: hereditary (dystrophies, congenital myopathies, metabolic, mitochondrial, channelopathies), inflammatory (polymyositis, dermatomyositis, immune-mediated necrotising myopathy, inclusion body myositis), toxic (statin myopathy, steroid myopathy, alcohol myopathy), endocrine (hypothyroid, hyperthyroid, Cushing's), infectious, and critical illness myopathy. Histopathology is the gold standard for distinguishing them: inflammatory myopathies show inflammatory cell infiltrates, muscular dystrophies show necrosis and regeneration with structural protein deficiency on immunostaining, metabolic myopathies show glycogen or lipid accumulation, and mitochondrial myopathies show ragged-red fibres on modified Gomori trichrome stain. The unspecified G72.9 code is used until this classification is complete. Myopathies fall under the care of neurology and, for inflammatory forms, rheumatology.

typesClassification.description: Progressive genetic disorders — Duchenne, Becker, facioscapulohumeral, limb-girdle — caused by mutations in structural muscle proteins such as dystrophin. Present from childhood or early adulthood with progressive weakness.
Immune-mediated disorders including polymyositis, dermatomyositis, and immune-mediated necrotising myopathy; usually subacute in onset in adults and respond to immunosuppression.

Caused by enzyme deficiencies (acid maltase/Pompe disease, McArdle disease, carnitine deficiency) or mitochondrial DNA mutations; often associated with exercise intolerance and cramps rather than fixed weakness.
Caused by statins, corticosteroids, alcohol, colchicine, hydroxychloroquine, and other drugs or toxins; typically reversible after the causative agent is removed.
Secondary to hypothyroidism, hyperthyroidism, Cushing's syndrome, hyperparathyroidism, or Addison's disease; proximal weakness that reverses fully when the hormonal abnormality is corrected.

primarySymptoms: Proximal limb-girdle weakness — difficulty rising from a low chair, climbing stairs, or stepping on and off a bus — as the characteristic distribution because proximal muscles are affected first in most myopathies.
Shoulder girdle weakness causing difficulty lifting arms above the head or overhead reaching, such as putting objects on a shelf or washing hair.
Muscle wasting and reduced muscle bulk, often first noticed in the upper arms, thighs, and buttocks as clothing becomes loose in those regions.
Exercise intolerance — muscles fatigue unusually quickly with modest exertion, and some metabolic myopathies produce severe cramps or dark urine (myoglobinuria) after exercise.
Muscle pain and tenderness at rest or with gentle pressure, particularly in inflammatory myopathies; less prominent in purely hereditary or endocrine forms.
Difficulty swallowing (dysphagia) when pharyngeal or oesophageal muscles are involved, particularly in dermatomyositis, polymyositis, and inclusion body myositis.
Neck flexor weakness — inability to lift the head off a pillow or to keep the head upright against gravity, a finding that narrows the differential significantly.
Falls, frequent tripping, and difficulty maintaining balance because thigh and hip girdle weakness impairs stability during normal walking.
Respiratory muscle weakness in severe or advanced cases, producing exertional breathlessness, orthopnoea, or nocturnal hypoventilation detectable as morning headache and daytime sleepiness.
Dark reddish-brown urine after exercise in metabolic myopathies indicating myoglobinuria, a sign of significant muscle breakdown requiring urgent assessment.
earlyWarningSigns: Difficulty rising from a chair without using the arms, particularly when this is new and progressive over weeks to months.
Unusual fatigue in the legs or arms after activities that were previously well-tolerated.
New-onset mild muscle aching or tenderness in the thighs or upper arms without a history of unusual exercise.
A slight stagger or widened gait that develops gradually.
emergencySigns: Passage of dark brown (port-wine) urine after exertion, indicating myoglobinuria from acute rhabdomyolysis, which can cause acute kidney failure and requires emergency assessment.
Progressive shortness of breath at rest or inability to lie flat, suggesting respiratory muscle involvement requiring urgent respiratory assessment.
Severe rapidly progressive generalised weakness over days to weeks in an adult, which may indicate inflammatory myopathy or paraneoplastic process requiring prompt investigation.
Swallowing difficulties with choking or aspiration episodes, which carry a high risk of aspiration pneumonia.

causes.cause: Genetic mutations in muscle structural proteins
causes.description: Mutations in the genes encoding dystrophin (Duchenne/Becker), dysferlin, calpain-3, emerin, lamins, and others disrupt the structural integrity of the sarcolemma and sarcomere, causing progressive fibre necrosis and replacement by fibrofatty tissue without effective regeneration.

In polymyositis and dermatomyositis, autoreactive T cells and autoantibodies (including myositis-specific antibodies such as anti-Jo-1, anti-Mi-2, anti-MDA5) attack muscle fibres and the muscle microvasculature, producing necrosis, inflammation, and progressive weakness that responds to immunosuppression.

Statins inhibit HMG-CoA reductase in muscle mitochondria, reducing coenzyme Q10 and disrupting mitochondrial energy production; in susceptible individuals this causes statin-induced myopathy ranging from mild myalgia to, rarely, severe necrotising myopathy with anti-HMGCR antibodies that may persist after statin discontinuation.

Thyroid hormones are essential for normal muscle protein synthesis and mitochondrial function; hypothyroidism reduces ATPase activity and slows muscle relaxation (causing myoedema), while hyperthyroidism increases protein catabolism. Cushing's syndrome causes steroid-induced atrophy predominantly in proximal type II muscle fibres.

Mutations in mitochondrial or nuclear DNA encoding oxidative phosphorylation subunits impair the cell's ability to generate ATP aerobically; muscles with high energy demands — especially proximal limb and ocular muscles — are affected first, causing exercise intolerance and fixed weakness.

Chronic heavy alcohol consumption causes mitochondrial dysfunction and direct sarcolemmal damage, producing a predominantly proximal myopathy that improves with abstinence. Corticosteroids cause atrophy of type II fast-twitch fibres with chronic high-dose use.

riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives of patients with hereditary myopathy have substantially elevated risk depending on the inheritance pattern — X-linked for Duchenne, autosomal dominant or recessive for most limb-girdle forms.

Statin myopathy affects approximately 5-10% of users to some degree; risk rises with higher doses, with interacting medications (fibrates, cytochrome P450 inhibitors), and in the elderly. A baseline CK helps distinguish new myopathy from pre-existing elevation.
Dermatomyositis and polymyositis co-occur with systemic lupus erythematosus, rheumatoid arthritis, and Sjogren's syndrome at rates far above chance, sharing pathogenic autoimmune mechanisms.

Dermatomyositis carries a roughly threefold increased risk of underlying malignancy, particularly ovarian, lung, and gastrointestinal cancers; systematic cancer screening is part of the initial workup in all adult dermatomyositis patients.
Consuming more than 100 g of alcohol daily for years produces alcoholic myopathy in a significant proportion of heavy drinkers; myopathy improves substantially with abstinence.
Untreated hypothyroidism is a reversible cause of proximal myopathy and elevated CK; thyroid function testing should be among the first investigations in new-onset proximal weakness.

Older adults are more susceptible to statin myopathy, critical illness myopathy in hospitalisation, and endocrine myopathy; sarcopenia also confounds the picture, making diagnosis and attribution more complex.

affectedDemographics: Male children and young adults with X-linked dystrophies (Duchenne and Becker)
Adults aged 40-60, particularly women, with inflammatory myopathies (polymyositis, dermatomyositis)
Older adults on statin therapy at risk of statin myopathy
Patients with autoimmune connective tissue diseases including lupus and rheumatoid arthritis
Patients with endocrine disorders — hypothyroidism, hyperthyroidism, or Cushing's syndrome
Adults admitted to intensive care, among whom critical illness myopathy develops in up to 30% of prolonged stays
diagnosisOverview: The workup for myopathy begins with a thorough history focusing on the pattern of weakness onset (acute, subacute, or chronic), distribution (proximal versus distal, symmetric versus asymmetric), functional impact, family history, medication list (particularly statins), and systemic symptoms suggesting autoimmunity or malignancy. Serum creatine kinase (CK) is the first laboratory test — marked elevation (over 10 times the upper limit of normal) suggests inflammatory, toxic, or dystrophic causes, while normal or mildly elevated CK is more typical of endocrine or metabolic myopathy. Thyroid function tests, comprehensive metabolic panel, inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP), and myositis-specific autoantibodies are obtained early. Electromyography (EMG) with nerve conduction studies distinguishes myopathic from neuropathic patterns and identifies irritability suggesting active inflammation. MRI of affected muscle groups guides muscle biopsy site selection and reveals patterns of inflammation, fat replacement, and oedema characteristic of specific diagnoses. Muscle biopsy — analysed with haematoxylin and eosin, modified Gomori trichrome, enzyme histochemistry, immunostaining for structural proteins, and electron microscopy — provides the definitive pathological classification. Genetic testing is essential when hereditary dystrophy is suspected, with next-generation sequencing panels increasingly replacing single-gene testing.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the degree of muscle damage; markedly elevated CK (>10x upper limit of normal) strongly suggests an inflammatory, toxic, or dystrophic myopathy and guides urgency of further workup.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results return within hours. Exercise in the 48 hours before the test can transiently raise CK, so a rest period before sampling improves accuracy.

diagnosticTests.test: Thyroid function tests and metabolic panel
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies endocrine causes (hypothyroidism, hyperthyroidism, Cushing's), electrolyte disturbances, and renal function; potentially reversible causes that must not be missed.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results available within a day and may immediately explain the myopathy without further invasive testing.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies anti-Jo-1, anti-Mi-2, anti-MDA5, anti-HMGCR, anti-SRP, and other antibodies that classify inflammatory and immune-mediated myopathies and predict clinical features and treatment response.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; results take 1-2 weeks from a specialist laboratory. A positive antibody directs specific treatment and cancer screening decisions.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes myopathic changes (small, brief, polyphasic motor units with early recruitment) from neurogenic changes; insertional activity and fibrillations indicate active muscle inflammation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Surface electrodes and thin needle electrodes are placed in representative muscles; the procedure takes 45-90 minutes and causes brief discomfort. Results are available the same day.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Maps the pattern of muscle involvement, identifies areas of active inflammation (oedema on STIR sequences), fatty replacement, and guides the ideal biopsy site to maximise diagnostic yield.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 30-60 minute MRI scan of the thighs, pelvis, or arms depending on clinical weakness pattern; no radiation, and usually no injection needed.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides definitive histopathological classification, distinguishing inflammatory infiltrates, structural protein deficiency, metabolic storage, mitochondrial changes, and fibre necrosis patterns.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A minor surgical procedure under local anaesthesia taking 30-60 minutes; a small incision is made over a chosen muscle (typically vastus lateralis or deltoid). Light discomfort and a small scar remain.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies pathogenic mutations in dystrophin, dysferlin, calpain-3, and other myopathy genes in hereditary cases, replacing incremental single-gene testing.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw; results take 4-12 weeks. Genetic counselling is offered alongside, particularly when implications for family members exist.

treatmentOverview: Treatment depends entirely on the specific type of myopathy identified. Inflammatory myopathies are treated with high-dose corticosteroids as initial therapy — typically prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day — followed by a steroid-sparing immunosuppressant such as azathioprine, methotrexate, mycophenolate, or for refractory cases, intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) or rituximab. Response is measured by CK normalisation, improvement in manual muscle strength, and functional measures; most patients achieve significant improvement within months. Toxic myopathy from statins resolves in the majority of cases within weeks to months of stopping the drug; immune-mediated necrotising myopathy from statins requires immunosuppression. Endocrine myopathies are treated by correcting the hormonal abnormality, with muscle strength returning over months. Hereditary dystrophies lack disease-modifying therapy for most forms, though exon-skipping gene therapies (eteplirsen for Duchenne) have regulatory approval and corticosteroids slow functional decline in Duchenne. All myopathies benefit from supervised physiotherapy to maintain and improve function, respiratory monitoring where respiratory muscles are at risk, and nutritional support to counteract catabolism.

medicalTreatments.name: Corticosteroids (prednisolone)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line immunosuppression for inflammatory myopathies; high-dose prednisolone (1 mg/kg/day up to 80 mg) is given initially to control active inflammation, then tapered slowly over 12-24 months to the minimum effective dose to limit side effects.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves clinical improvement in approximately 70-80% of polymyositis and dermatomyositis patients within 3-6 months; CK typically normalises before strength recovers.

medicalTreatments.name: Azathioprine
medicalTreatments.description: A steroid-sparing immunosuppressant given alongside prednisolone in inflammatory myopathy to allow a lower corticosteroid dose and reduce cumulative steroid toxicity; requires thiopurine methyltransferase (TPMT) testing before prescribing to avoid severe toxicity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces relapse rates and steroid requirements; approximately 60-70% of inflammatory myopathy patients achieve sustained remission on prednisolone plus azathioprine.

medicalTreatments.name: Methotrexate
medicalTreatments.description: An alternative steroid-sparing agent used in inflammatory myopathy and with particular efficacy in dermatomyositis skin disease; given weekly with folic acid supplementation to reduce mucositis and bone marrow suppression.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable to azathioprine for muscle disease control; approximately 60-65% of patients respond to methotrexate as an immunosuppressive adjunct.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG)
medicalTreatments.description: Pooled immunoglobulin infusions given monthly or every 2-3 months modulate immune activation; used in refractory dermatomyositis and polymyositis, and in acute deterioration. A randomised trial demonstrated efficacy in refractory dermatomyositis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 70% of refractory dermatomyositis patients show improvement in strength and skin disease with IVIG in randomised and cohort data.

medicalTreatments.name: Thyroid hormone replacement
medicalTreatments.description: For hypothyroid myopathy, levothyroxine correction of the thyroid deficiency fully reverses the proximal weakness, elevated CK, and myoedema over 6-12 weeks — a drug treatment that cures the myopathy rather than managing it.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Near-complete resolution of myopathy in essentially 100% of patients with pure hypothyroid myopathy when euthyroidism is restored.

alternativeTreatments.name: Supervised physiotherapy and exercise
alternativeTreatments.description: Controlled resistance and aerobic exercise programmes have been shown in multiple randomised trials to improve muscle strength and physical function in inflammatory myopathies without triggering flares; weekly supervised sessions with a physiotherapist experienced in myopathy are recommended.

alternativeTreatments.name: Nutritional support and supplementation
alternativeTreatments.description: Adequate protein intake (at least 1.2 g/kg/day) helps counter muscle catabolism from inflammation and corticosteroid use; vitamin D and calcium supplementation is essential for patients on long-term corticosteroids to prevent steroid-induced osteoporosis.

alternativeTreatments.name: Coenzyme Q10 supplementation
alternativeTreatments.description: Coenzyme Q10 is depleted by statin treatment; supplementation may reduce statin-associated myalgia in some patients, though trial data show modest and inconsistent effects. Mitochondrial myopathy patients often receive CoQ10 as adjunctive therapy.

whySeeSpecialist: A neurologist or rheumatologist with expertise in neuromuscular disease should evaluate all patients with suspected myopathy. Accurate diagnosis requires specialist-guided muscle biopsy, interpretation of myositis autoantibody panels, and access to EMG. Missing a treatable cause — inflammatory myopathy, endocrine myopathy, drug toxicity — leads to progressive avoidable weakness. Adults with new dermatomyositis need specialist-led cancer screening. Patients with hereditary dystrophies benefit from multidisciplinary neuromuscular clinics providing respiratory monitoring, genetic counselling, and access to gene therapy trials.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a neurologist with subspecialty training in neuromuscular disease or a rheumatologist experienced in inflammatory myopathies, ideally at a centre with on-site EMG, muscle biopsy processing, and a dedicated neuromuscular multidisciplinary team. Access to advanced genetic testing and clinical trials for hereditary forms is an advantage. For inflammatory myopathy, enquire whether the centre participates in systematic cancer surveillance and autoantibody-guided treatment protocols.
preventionStrategies: Monitor creatine kinase at baseline and periodically in patients starting statins, particularly at high doses or with known risk factors, to detect early statin myopathy.
Correct thyroid, parathyroid, and adrenal disorders promptly; endocrine myopathies are fully reversible when the hormonal cause is treated early.
Minimise corticosteroid doses and duration in patients with inflammatory disease; use steroid-sparing agents as early as possible to prevent steroid-induced myopathy.
Limit chronic heavy alcohol consumption, which is both a direct myotoxin and compounds nutritional deficiencies that worsen muscle health.
Genetic counselling for families with confirmed hereditary dystrophy enables informed reproductive decision-making and early diagnosis in at-risk relatives.
lifestyleModifications: Remain as physically active as the disease allows — supervised exercise improves strength and reduces fatigue in inflammatory myopathy and does not worsen disease activity.
Pace daily activities to conserve energy; rest periods between physical tasks help manage fatigue without abandoning function.
Maintain a high-protein, anti-inflammatory diet to support muscle repair; adequate calories are essential because malnutrition accelerates muscle loss.
Ensure adequate vitamin D levels through supplementation, particularly during corticosteroid therapy, to protect bone and muscle function.
Avoid alcohol and unnecessary over-the-counter medications with known myotoxicity (including colchicine and hydroxychloroquine at high doses) unless specifically prescribed.

recommended: High-quality protein from fish, lean poultry, eggs, and legumes (minimum 1.2 g/kg/day) to support muscle protein synthesis
Omega-3 fatty acids from oily fish or fish-oil supplements for anti-inflammatory effects
Adequate calcium (1,000-1,200 mg/day) and vitamin D (800-1,000 IU/day) during corticosteroid therapy
A Mediterranean-style diet rich in vegetables, fruit, whole grains, and olive oil to reduce systemic inflammation
avoid: Chronic heavy alcohol, which is directly myotoxic and impairs muscle protein synthesis
Very low-calorie diets or fasting during active inflammatory myopathy, which accelerates muscle catabolism
High-dose grapefruit juice in patients on statins, as it inhibits CYP3A4 and increases statin levels and myopathy risk

exerciseGuidelines: Supervised exercise is beneficial and recommended for most myopathy patients. Randomised trials show resistance training 2-3 times weekly combined with aerobic exercise improves strength and reduces fatigue in inflammatory myopathy without increasing disease activity or CK. Exercise intensity should be guided by a physiotherapist with neuromuscular expertise; patients with respiratory compromise or unstable disease require medical clearance first. Complete rest is counterproductive and accelerates deconditioning.
dailyManagement: Take immunosuppressive medications consistently and do not miss doses; abrupt steroid discontinuation can precipitate adrenal crisis.
Monitor for signs of infection, which is the leading cause of morbidity in patients on corticosteroids and immunosuppressants.
Track functional milestones — ability to rise from a chair, climb stairs, or lift objects — to detect relapse early between clinic visits.
Report new swallowing difficulties promptly; dysphagia in myopathy carries aspiration risk.
Ensure regular bone density monitoring (DEXA scan) in patients on long-term corticosteroids; bisphosphonates are often co-prescribed for prevention.
prognosis: Prognosis varies greatly by myopathy type. Inflammatory myopathies achieve sustained remission in approximately 60-80% of patients with appropriate immunosuppression, though a subset develop treatment-resistant or relapsing disease. Endocrine myopathies fully recover with hormonal correction. Statin myopathy resolves completely in most patients within months of stopping the drug. Hereditary dystrophies are progressive and cause significant long-term disability; Duchenne muscular dystrophy with modern cardiorespiratory management now has median survival extending into the third or fourth decade. Inclusion body myositis is slowly progressive, refractory to immunosuppression, and may require walking aids within 10-15 years of diagnosis. Inflammatory myopathy associated with malignancy has a prognosis tied to the cancer. Critical illness myopathy largely resolves with recovery, though some patients retain residual weakness.
recoveryTimeline: Endocrine and statin myopathies show CK normalisation within 4-12 weeks of correcting the cause, with strength improving over 3-6 months. Inflammatory myopathies respond to treatment over 3-6 months; full functional recovery may take 12-24 months, and relapses requiring treatment adjustment occur in 30-50% of patients. Hereditary dystrophies do not recover — the goal is slowing progression and maintaining function. Critical illness myopathy typically shows gradual improvement over weeks to months of rehabilitation.
complications: Respiratory failure from diaphragm and intercostal muscle involvement in advanced dystrophy or severe inflammatory myopathy, requiring ventilatory support.
Aspiration pneumonia secondary to pharyngeal and oesophageal muscle weakness and dysphagia, particularly in dermatomyositis and inclusion body myositis.
Acute kidney injury from myoglobinuria in severe rhabdomyolysis, requiring aggressive fluid resuscitation to prevent renal tubular damage.
Steroid-induced osteoporosis and vertebral fractures in patients on long-term corticosteroid therapy for inflammatory myopathy.
Cardiomyopathy and arrhythmias in Duchenne and Emery-Dreifuss muscular dystrophy, as cardiac muscle is involved alongside skeletal muscle.
Social isolation and depression from progressive functional limitation; psychological support is an important component of care.

supportResources.name: Muscular Dystrophy Association (MDA)
supportResources.description: U.S. non-profit providing patient support, care centers, and research funding for muscular dystrophies and all neuromuscular diseases including myopathies.

supportResources.name: Myositis Association
supportResources.description: Patient advocacy and support organisation specifically for inflammatory myopathies, offering educational resources, webinars, and peer support communities.

supportResources.name: NINDS — Myopathy Information
supportResources.description: Evidence-based patient information on myopathy types, causes, and treatment from the U.S. National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke.

sources.title: Dalakas MC. Inflammatory muscle diseases. New England Journal of Medicine, 2015

Lundberg IE et al. 2017 European League Against Rheumatism/American College of Rheumatology classification criteria for adult and juvenile idiopathic inflammatory myopathies. Annals of the Rheumatic Diseases, 2017
Mammen AL. Statin-associated autoimmune myopathy. NEJM, 2016

Griggs RC et al. Corticosteroids in Duchenne muscular dystrophy. Muscle & Nerve, 2016

confusedWithConditions.name: Multiple sclerosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: MS produces a mixed upper and lower motor neuron pattern with spasticity, hyperreflexia, sensory loss, and MRI white matter lesions; myopathy causes pure lower motor neuron weakness, preserved or reduced reflexes, and no sensory deficit.

confusedWithConditions.name: Polymyositis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Polymyositis is a specific subtype of inflammatory myopathy defined by T-cell-mediated muscle inflammation without skin involvement; 'myopathy unspecified' is the broader category used before the subtype is confirmed.

confusedWithConditions.name: Myasthenia gravis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Myasthenia gravis is a neuromuscular junction disorder causing fluctuating, fatigable weakness — particularly of ocular and bulbar muscles — with normal CK, normal EMG apart from decremental response, and positive acetylcholine receptor antibodies.

relatedConditions.name: Dermatomyositis
relatedConditions.relevance: An inflammatory myopathy subtype with characteristic skin involvement; shares pathophysiology and management principles with other inflammatory myopathies.

relatedConditions.name: Polymyositis
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common pure inflammatory myopathy subtype; frequently considered in the differential before a specific diagnosis is established.

relatedConditions.name: Muscular dystrophy
relatedConditions.relevance: The principal hereditary myopathy group; inherited progressive muscle disease is an important cause of myopathy unspecified awaiting genetic classification.

relatedConditions.name: Rhabdomyolysis
relatedConditions.relevance: Severe acute muscle breakdown from myopathy, extreme exercise, trauma, or toxins; shares elevated CK and myoglobinuria with metabolic and toxic myopathies.

relatedConditions.name: Inclusion body myositis
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common inflammatory myopathy in adults over 50; characterised by distal and proximal weakness and profound refractoriness to immunosuppression.

faqs.question: What is myopathy?
faqs.answer: Myopathy is a disease of skeletal muscle itself, not of the nerves controlling it. The muscle fibres are damaged, inflamed, or structurally abnormal, causing weakness, wasting, and fatigue predominantly in the shoulder and hip muscles.

faqs.question: What are the main symptoms of myopathy?
faqs.answer: The main symptoms are proximal muscle weakness — difficulty rising from a chair, climbing stairs, or lifting arms overhead — together with muscle fatigue, aching, and sometimes muscle wasting. Reflexes and sensation are usually preserved, unlike in nerve disease.

faqs.question: What causes myopathy?
faqs.answer: Myopathy can be inherited (muscular dystrophies, metabolic myopathies), caused by autoimmune inflammation (polymyositis, dermatomyositis), triggered by drugs (statins, steroids, alcohol), or result from hormonal disorders (hypothyroidism, Cushing's syndrome).

faqs.question: How is myopathy diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis starts with blood tests for CK, thyroid function, and myositis antibodies, followed by EMG and nerve conduction studies. Muscle MRI identifies affected areas. A muscle biopsy provides the definitive histopathological classification when the cause remains uncertain.

faqs.question: Is myopathy the same as muscular dystrophy?
faqs.answer: Muscular dystrophy is a subset of myopathy — it refers specifically to inherited, progressive myopathies caused by mutations in muscle structural proteins. Myopathy is the broader term covering all primary muscle diseases, including inflammatory, toxic, and endocrine forms.

faqs.question: Can myopathy be cured?
faqs.answer: It depends on the type. Endocrine myopathies cure completely when the hormonal abnormality is corrected. Statin myopathy resolves after stopping the drug. Inflammatory myopathies can achieve sustained remission with immunosuppression. Hereditary dystrophies are not curable but can be managed.

faqs.question: Do statins cause myopathy?
faqs.answer: Yes. Statin-associated myopathy affects approximately 5-10% of statin users, ranging from mild muscle aching to, rarely, severe immune-mediated necrotising myopathy. Mild cases resolve after stopping the statin; immune-mediated forms require immunosuppression even after the drug is discontinued.

faqs.question: What is CK and why is it measured in myopathy?
faqs.answer: Creatine kinase is an enzyme released into the blood when muscle fibres are damaged. A markedly elevated CK (more than 10 times normal) strongly suggests active muscle disease and helps guide urgency of further investigation. CK levels also track disease activity during treatment.

faqs.question: Can exercise make myopathy worse?
faqs.answer: For most myopathies, supervised moderate exercise improves strength and function without worsening disease. Inflammatory myopathy studies show exercise does not raise CK or increase inflammation. Extreme exertion can trigger rhabdomyolysis in metabolic myopathies, so pacing and medical guidance are important.

faqs.question: What is inflammatory myopathy?
faqs.answer: Inflammatory myopathy is a group of autoimmune muscle diseases — including polymyositis, dermatomyositis, and immune-mediated necrotising myopathy — in which the immune system attacks muscle tissue. They cause subacute proximal weakness, elevated CK, and respond to immunosuppressive treatment.

faqs.question: Is myopathy hereditary?
faqs.answer: Some forms are. Muscular dystrophies and congenital myopathies are inherited and follow autosomal dominant, autosomal recessive, or X-linked patterns. Inflammatory, endocrine, and toxic myopathies are generally not inherited, though genetic susceptibility may increase risk.

faqs.question: How is inflammatory myopathy treated?
faqs.answer: First-line treatment is high-dose corticosteroids (prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day) combined with a steroid-sparing immunosuppressant such as azathioprine or methotrexate. Approximately 70-80% of patients achieve significant improvement. Refractory cases may receive IVIG or rituximab.

faqs.question: Can myopathy affect breathing?
faqs.answer: Yes, in severe or advanced cases. Respiratory muscle involvement causes breathlessness, inability to lie flat, and nocturnal hypoventilation. Regular respiratory function testing is part of monitoring in neuromuscular disease, and ventilatory support may be needed.

faqs.question: What is the difference between myopathy and neuropathy?
faqs.answer: Myopathy is disease of the muscle itself, causing proximal weakness with preserved reflexes and normal sensation. Neuropathy is disease of the peripheral nerves, causing weakness with reduced reflexes and sensory loss typically in a length-dependent (stocking-glove) pattern. EMG distinguishes them.

faqs.question: Does dermatomyositis increase cancer risk?
faqs.answer: Yes. Dermatomyositis carries an approximately threefold increased risk of underlying malignancy, particularly ovarian, lung, gastric, and colorectal cancers. All adult patients with new dermatomyositis receive systematic cancer screening including CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis.

faqs.question: What does myoglobinuria mean?
faqs.answer: Myoglobinuria means myoglobin — a protein released when muscle fibres break down — is appearing in the urine, turning it brown or red. It indicates significant muscle damage (rhabdomyolysis) and can damage the kidneys. Dark urine after exertion requires urgent medical assessment.

faqs.question: How long does recovery from statin myopathy take?
faqs.answer: Most mild statin myopathy resolves within 4-12 weeks of stopping the statin. CK normalises before strength fully returns. Immune-mediated necrotising myopathy from statins does not resolve spontaneously and requires months of immunosuppressive treatment.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor for muscle weakness?
faqs.answer: See a doctor promptly if you have progressive difficulty rising from a chair, climbing stairs, or lifting your arms, especially if it develops over weeks to months. Urgent assessment is needed for dark urine after exertion, sudden severe weakness, or weakness with swallowing difficulty.

faqs.question: Is myopathy the same as fibromyalgia?
faqs.answer: No. Fibromyalgia causes widespread pain and fatigue without objective muscle weakness, elevated CK, or abnormal muscle biopsy. Myopathy produces measurable weakness, may cause CK elevation, and has definable structural or inflammatory pathology on biopsy. The conditions can coexist but are distinct.

faqs.question: Can hypothyroidism cause myopathy?
faqs.answer: Yes. Hypothyroidism is a reversible cause of proximal weakness and elevated CK. The myopathy resolves completely when thyroid hormone replacement restores euthyroidism, usually within 6-12 weeks of starting treatment. Thyroid function testing is among the first steps in evaluating new proximal weakness.

metaDescription: Myopathy causes proximal muscle weakness, fatigue and wasting. Learn about inflammatory, hereditary and toxic types, diagnosis and treatment. Doctor-reviewed.
